title: Gender Equity of Perceived Career and Influence Opportunities among Political Elites: The Case of Swedish Parliamentarians
abstract: Under which conditions is gender equity accomplished in terms of career and influence opportunities among elites? We hypothesize that an equal distribution of home/family sphere work demands, in combination with equal levels of person–environment ‘fit’ between men and women in the organization, contributes to gender equity in terms of (intra-organizational) career success, in turn enabling gender equity of influence possibilities among elite-positioned individuals. We further argue that for such equal gender distributions to occur in the first place, a welfare state setting that encourages equal divisions of private/public labour between the genders (i), and an organization containing a ‘balanced’ gender group proportion (ii), need to be evident. The theoretical propositions are integrated into a conceptual model and its individual-level dimensions are then tested empirically. We use a unique dataset covering one of the few existing elite populations situated in a context where the two identified macro conditions (i and ii) are evident. The model is tested on survey data from a total population of Swedish National Parliamentarians (with a remarkably high 83 per cent response rate), using structural equation modelling (SEM). The model fits well with data (P = 0.23) and the main hypotheses are confirmed. Adding additional control variables does not change the initial results.
